{
  "id": "w-mo3depkw-0d1587e47150",
  "type": "brief",
  "status": "open",
  "title": "Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`",
  "body": "# Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`

## Intent

Wire the Spider so that writ dispatch can be gated on outbound links of a load-bearing kind that expresses precedence. A writ that depends on others is held until those others reach a terminal state; success releases the gate, non-success terminal states put the dependent into `stuck`. Recovery (a previously failed dependency eventually succeeding) auto-unsticks the dependent. Cycles in the dependency graph are detected by Spider and surfaced as a stuck condition. This is the first real consumer of the link-kind substrate and the first writer of the per-plugin observation slot on writs.

## Motivation

Two pieces of substrate have been landed in prior commissions — the link-meaning layer (renamed to link-*kinds* with `label`/`kind` fields) and the per-plugin observation slot on writs (`status.<pluginId>`). Both are in place but unused. No plugin currently reacts to a kind. No plugin currently writes into the observation slot.

The Spider currently dispatches every `open` writ of a dispatchable type as soon as concurrency allows, with no awareness of relationships between writs. Any real workflow with ordering (build before deploy, parent before child wrap-up, one commission depends on another, etc.) has to rely on the operator authoring writs in a specific sequence and never benefits from the substrate that exists for exactly this purpose.

Spider is the natural first consumer because it already owns the dispatch decision. The questions that needed answering — composition, re-evaluation mechanism, cycle handling, recovery semantics, provenance — were worked through in the click subtree at `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385`.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### The kind: `spider.follows`

A new link-kind contributed by the Spider plugin.

**Description (verbatim):** *"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."*

The kind is a pure temporal-ordering contract. Spider's specific policy (stuck-on-failure, auto-unstick on recovery) is its own consumer-side behavior, not part of the kind's meaning. A future consumer with different recovery semantics could legitimately bind to the same kind.

Spider registers the kind through the existing `supportKit.linkKinds` contribution mechanism; Clerk consumes it through the existing kit substrate.

### Composition: conjunctive

Multiple outbound `spider.follows` links from a single writ are conjunctive — *all* targets must release before the dependent dispatches. Any single non-terminal blocker keeps the gate closed; any single failed blocker puts the dependent into `stuck`.

### Direction: outbound from the dependent

A writ's own dependencies are its *outbound* `spider.follows` links (source → target means "source depends on target"). Spider reads the candidate writ's outbound links and inspects the target writs' phases. "Inbound on the target" and "outbound on the source" are the same edges; the outbound reading matches the directional semantics and avoids inverting the kind's meaning at the read site.

### Re-evaluation: Spider's poll

Gate state is re-evaluated on Spider's existing crawl loop. No new event-dispatch substrate is required. The same poll that picks up newly-eligible writs also re-walks the dependencies of currently-gated and currently-stuck writs.

### Cycle handling: Spider detects, Clerk stays thin

Cycles in the `spider.follows` graph are detected by Spider during gate evaluation, not prevented by Clerk at link-creation. Clerk remains link-kind-agnostic. Cycle detection runs per-evaluation (during the same graph walk that evaluates gates), not as a separate periodic scan. A cycle puts every writ in the cycle into `stuck` with a clear cycle-related reason; cycles are recoverable (when one member transitions out via external action, the others auto-unstick on the next poll).

### Auto-unstick on recovery

When a previously-failed blocker eventually reaches success (typically via operator-driven retry), Spider auto-unsticks the gated dependent on the next poll, returning it to `open`. Rationale: the operator's action *was* fixing the dependency; requiring a second manual unstick step is redundant ceremony. The gated state is purely derived from blocker state; once the cause is resolved, the symptom is resolved.

### Provenance: Spider records its own causes

Spider only auto-unsticks writs whose stuck transition Spider itself authored. The mechanism is the per-plugin observation slot established in the prior status-convention commission: Spider writes its stuck-cause provenance into its own sub-slot (`status.spider`). This cleanly separates Spider-authored stucks from stucks caused by other actors (operator, future plugins, engine cascade), which Spider will not touch.

The engine-cascade stuck path (when a rig's engine fails and Spider transitions the writ to stuck with a resolution) does *not* write into the Spider sub-slot. Absence-of-cause on a stuck writ uniquely means "not Spider's gating doing this."

### Terminal-state handling on blockers

| Blocker state | Gate behavior |
|---|---|
| `completed` | Release the gate (contributes to the "all blockers terminal and successful" predicate). |
| `cancelled` | Treated the same as `completed` for gate-release purposes. Cancellation is an operator-chosen terminal outcome; dependent writs should not stuck-cascade from it. |
| `failed` | Cascade to `stuck` on the dependent with `stuckCause = 'failed-blocker'`. |
| Any non-terminal (`new`, `open`, `stuck`) | Gate remains closed; dependent stays `open`. |

### Stuck cascade scope: direct dependents only

When a blocker ends in `failed`, Spider transitions only the *directly* dependent writ to `stuck`. Transitive cascade (sticking writs that depend on the newly-stuck one) is not done as a single atomic pass — the next Spider poll naturally evaluates those writs against their own blockers and sticks them through the same mechanism. This keeps each per-poll action scoped to one edge.

### Stuck reason text

Operator-facing `resolution` text on a Spider-stuck writ is human-readable and names the specific blockers by short id:

- *"Blocked by failed dependency: w-abc123"*
- *"Blocked by failed dependencies: w-abc123, w-def456"* (multiple)
- *"Cycle detected in spider.follows graph"*

The structured detail (which blockers, which phases, when observed) lives in `status.spider` — `resolution` is the human summary; the status slot is the machine-readable truth.

### Lifecycle of a gated writ

Gating happens entirely from `open`. The `new` phase means draft (author still amending) and is out of scope for Spider gating.

| Transition | Trigger |
|---|---|
| `new` (draft) — out of scope | Author amending; Spider does not touch. |
| `open` (gated) — Spider sees, skips dispatch | Outbound `spider.follows` blocker is non-terminal. |
| `open` → dispatched | All blockers in terminal success states (completed or cancelled). |
| `open` → `stuck` (failed dependency) | A blocker reached `failed`. Spider writes `status.spider.stuckCause = 'failed-blocker'` and the human resolution text. |
| `open` → `stuck` (cycle) | Spider detected a cycle. All cycle members stuck with `stuckCause = 'cycle'`. |
| `stuck` → `open` (auto-unstick) | All previously-failed blockers eventually reached terminal success (or the cycle was broken). Spider clears its `status.spider.stuckCause`. |

A known visibility gap: `open` overloads "finalized-and-waiting-on-gate", "finalized-and-eligible", and "actively-running". Captured separately at `c-mo301yp9` (parked) for future surfacing work — likely an Oculus column reading from the Spider status sub-slot.

### Authoring surfaces expose both `label` and `kind`

Wherever a link can be created interactively, both fields are first-class inputs with the right control type for each. This commission completes the Oculus side of that story:

- **CLI.** `nsg writ link` already exposes both `label` and `--kind` (landed with the link-substrate rename commission). No further CLI work here.
- **Oculus writ page.** The inline add-link form today exposes only the casual label input. This commission adds a `kind` dropdown sourced from the kit-contributed registry (`listKinds()`). Operators do not type kind ids by hand. The label input and its autocomplete datalist are preserved; the dropdown is additive. When the operator submits a link, if the kind is rejected by Clerk (unknown/unregistered) the error surfaces inline on the form, not as a page-level failure.

Authoring with `kind` omitted creates a documentary link (`kind = null`); this is the casual-tagging path and remains supported.

## Out of scope

- **Database / data migration of existing data.** Handled out of band by the patron. No existing links carry `kind = 'spider.follows'`; a retroactive cycle scan is moot.
- **New CLI command for `spider.follows`.** The existing `nsg writ link` is the authoring surface — no Spider-specific command.
- **A richer link-authoring UI in Oculus** (writ picker with search, kind-aware suggestions, etc.). The existing inline add-link form on the writ page is extended to add the `kind` dropdown; no broader UX redesign.
- **Oculus rendering of the Spider sub-slot** on the writ table (e.g. a gate column). Natural next step but a separate commission.
- **Introducing the status convention on non-writ runtime objects.** The slot remains writ-only in this commission. Rigs, engines, sessions adopt it when their first consumer needs it.
- **Closing the open-phase visibility gap.** Captured at `c-mo301yp9` (parked).
- **Hopper concurrency interactions.** Gate evaluation is upstream of concurrency; no change to existing concurrency limits or scheduler behavior.
- **Event-dispatch substrate / Clockworks integration.** The polling decision is deliberate; this commission does not introduce or rely on event-dispatch substrate.
- **Any change to the existing engine-cascade stuck path** (where a rig's engine failure transitions the writ to stuck). That path is untouched; Spider's auto-unstick logic simply skips writs whose sub-slot cause is absent.

## References

- **Design subtree** (this commission): click `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385` and its concluded children.
- **Prior substrate commissions this one depends on** (both dispatched separately before this one):
  - Link-substrate rename sweep — introduces the `kind` field, `listKinds()`, and the dot-form id separator that `spider.follows` uses.
  - Status convention on writs — introduces the `status: Record<PluginId, unknown>` observation slot that `status.spider` lives in.
- **Status-convention design** (referenced by the prior brief; relevant here for the sub-slot semantics Spider consumes): click `c-mo33duvq-7f774446c6c9` and its concluded children.
- **Final kind naming** (`spider.follows`): click `c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3` (name + description), with the id-format adjustment at `c-mo3465sf-d9187bc9322a`.
- **Visibility-gap follow-up** (parked): click `c-mo301yp9-c186db746a77`.",
  "codex": "nexus",
  "createdAt": "2026-04-17T20:38:26.048Z",
  "updatedAt": "2026-04-17T20:38:26.048Z",
  "links": {
    "outbound": [],
    "inbound": []
  },
  "parent": null,
  "children": {
    "summary": {},
    "items": []
  }
}

[
  {
    "id": "w-mo3depkw-0d1587e47150",
    "type": "brief",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`",
    "body": "# Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`

## Intent

Wire the Spider so that writ dispatch can be gated on outbound links of a load-bearing kind that expresses precedence. A writ that depends on others is held until those others reach a terminal state; success releases the gate, non-success terminal states put the dependent into `stuck`. Recovery (a previously failed dependency eventually succeeding) auto-unsticks the dependent. Cycles in the dependency graph are detected by Spider and surfaced as a stuck condition. This is the first real consumer of the link-kind substrate and the first writer of the per-plugin observation slot on writs.

## Motivation

Two pieces of substrate have been landed in prior commissions — the link-meaning layer (renamed to link-*kinds* with `label`/`kind` fields) and the per-plugin observation slot on writs (`status.<pluginId>`). Both are in place but unused. No plugin currently reacts to a kind. No plugin currently writes into the observation slot.

The Spider currently dispatches every `open` writ of a dispatchable type as soon as concurrency allows, with no awareness of relationships between writs. Any real workflow with ordering (build before deploy, parent before child wrap-up, one commission depends on another, etc.) has to rely on the operator authoring writs in a specific sequence and never benefits from the substrate that exists for exactly this purpose.

Spider is the natural first consumer because it already owns the dispatch decision. The questions that needed answering — composition, re-evaluation mechanism, cycle handling, recovery semantics, provenance — were worked through in the click subtree at `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385`.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### The kind: `spider.follows`

A new link-kind contributed by the Spider plugin.

**Description (verbatim):** *"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."*

The kind is a pure temporal-ordering contract. Spider's specific policy (stuck-on-failure, auto-unstick on recovery) is its own consumer-side behavior, not part of the kind's meaning. A future consumer with different recovery semantics could legitimately bind to the same kind.

Spider registers the kind through the existing `supportKit.linkKinds` contribution mechanism; Clerk consumes it through the existing kit substrate.

### Composition: conjunctive

Multiple outbound `spider.follows` links from a single writ are conjunctive — *all* targets must release before the dependent dispatches. Any single non-terminal blocker keeps the gate closed; any single failed blocker puts the dependent into `stuck`.

### Direction: outbound from the dependent

A writ's own dependencies are its *outbound* `spider.follows` links (source → target means "source depends on target"). Spider reads the candidate writ's outbound links and inspects the target writs' phases. "Inbound on the target" and "outbound on the source" are the same edges; the outbound reading matches the directional semantics and avoids inverting the kind's meaning at the read site.

### Re-evaluation: Spider's poll

Gate state is re-evaluated on Spider's existing crawl loop. No new event-dispatch substrate is required. The same poll that picks up newly-eligible writs also re-walks the dependencies of currently-gated and currently-stuck writs.

### Cycle handling: Spider detects, Clerk stays thin

Cycles in the `spider.follows` graph are detected by Spider during gate evaluation, not prevented by Clerk at link-creation. Clerk remains link-kind-agnostic. Cycle detection runs per-evaluation (during the same graph walk that evaluates gates), not as a separate periodic scan. A cycle puts every writ in the cycle into `stuck` with a clear cycle-related reason; cycles are recoverable (when one member transitions out via external action, the others auto-unstick on the next poll).

### Auto-unstick on recovery

When a previously-failed blocker eventually reaches success (typically via operator-driven retry), Spider auto-unsticks the gated dependent on the next poll, returning it to `open`. Rationale: the operator's action *was* fixing the dependency; requiring a second manual unstick step is redundant ceremony. The gated state is purely derived from blocker state; once the cause is resolved, the symptom is resolved.

### Provenance: Spider records its own causes

Spider only auto-unsticks writs whose stuck transition Spider itself authored. The mechanism is the per-plugin observation slot established in the prior status-convention commission: Spider writes its stuck-cause provenance into its own sub-slot (`status.spider`). This cleanly separates Spider-authored stucks from stucks caused by other actors (operator, future plugins, engine cascade), which Spider will not touch.

The engine-cascade stuck path (when a rig's engine fails and Spider transitions the writ to stuck with a resolution) does *not* write into the Spider sub-slot. Absence-of-cause on a stuck writ uniquely means "not Spider's gating doing this."

### Terminal-state handling on blockers

| Blocker state | Gate behavior |
|---|---|
| `completed` | Release the gate (contributes to the "all blockers terminal and successful" predicate). |
| `cancelled` | Treated the same as `completed` for gate-release purposes. Cancellation is an operator-chosen terminal outcome; dependent writs should not stuck-cascade from it. |
| `failed` | Cascade to `stuck` on the dependent with `stuckCause = 'failed-blocker'`. |
| Any non-terminal (`new`, `open`, `stuck`) | Gate remains closed; dependent stays `open`. |

### Stuck cascade scope: direct dependents only

When a blocker ends in `failed`, Spider transitions only the *directly* dependent writ to `stuck`. Transitive cascade (sticking writs that depend on the newly-stuck one) is not done as a single atomic pass — the next Spider poll naturally evaluates those writs against their own blockers and sticks them through the same mechanism. This keeps each per-poll action scoped to one edge.

### Stuck reason text

Operator-facing `resolution` text on a Spider-stuck writ is human-readable and names the specific blockers by short id:

- *"Blocked by failed dependency: w-abc123"*
- *"Blocked by failed dependencies: w-abc123, w-def456"* (multiple)
- *"Cycle detected in spider.follows graph"*

The structured detail (which blockers, which phases, when observed) lives in `status.spider` — `resolution` is the human summary; the status slot is the machine-readable truth.

### Lifecycle of a gated writ

Gating happens entirely from `open`. The `new` phase means draft (author still amending) and is out of scope for Spider gating.

| Transition | Trigger |
|---|---|
| `new` (draft) — out of scope | Author amending; Spider does not touch. |
| `open` (gated) — Spider sees, skips dispatch | Outbound `spider.follows` blocker is non-terminal. |
| `open` → dispatched | All blockers in terminal success states (completed or cancelled). |
| `open` → `stuck` (failed dependency) | A blocker reached `failed`. Spider writes `status.spider.stuckCause = 'failed-blocker'` and the human resolution text. |
| `open` → `stuck` (cycle) | Spider detected a cycle. All cycle members stuck with `stuckCause = 'cycle'`. |
| `stuck` → `open` (auto-unstick) | All previously-failed blockers eventually reached terminal success (or the cycle was broken). Spider clears its `status.spider.stuckCause`. |

A known visibility gap: `open` overloads "finalized-and-waiting-on-gate", "finalized-and-eligible", and "actively-running". Captured separately at `c-mo301yp9` (parked) for future surfacing work — likely an Oculus column reading from the Spider status sub-slot.

### Authoring surfaces expose both `label` and `kind`

Wherever a link can be created interactively, both fields are first-class inputs with the right control type for each. This commission completes the Oculus side of that story:

- **CLI.** `nsg writ link` already exposes both `label` and `--kind` (landed with the link-substrate rename commission). No further CLI work here.
- **Oculus writ page.** The inline add-link form today exposes only the casual label input. This commission adds a `kind` dropdown sourced from the kit-contributed registry (`listKinds()`). Operators do not type kind ids by hand. The label input and its autocomplete datalist are preserved; the dropdown is additive. When the operator submits a link, if the kind is rejected by Clerk (unknown/unregistered) the error surfaces inline on the form, not as a page-level failure.

Authoring with `kind` omitted creates a documentary link (`kind = null`); this is the casual-tagging path and remains supported.

## Out of scope

- **Database / data migration of existing data.** Handled out of band by the patron. No existing links carry `kind = 'spider.follows'`; a retroactive cycle scan is moot.
- **New CLI command for `spider.follows`.** The existing `nsg writ link` is the authoring surface — no Spider-specific command.
- **A richer link-authoring UI in Oculus** (writ picker with search, kind-aware suggestions, etc.). The existing inline add-link form on the writ page is extended to add the `kind` dropdown; no broader UX redesign.
- **Oculus rendering of the Spider sub-slot** on the writ table (e.g. a gate column). Natural next step but a separate commission.
- **Introducing the status convention on non-writ runtime objects.** The slot remains writ-only in this commission. Rigs, engines, sessions adopt it when their first consumer needs it.
- **Closing the open-phase visibility gap.** Captured at `c-mo301yp9` (parked).
- **Hopper concurrency interactions.** Gate evaluation is upstream of concurrency; no change to existing concurrency limits or scheduler behavior.
- **Event-dispatch substrate / Clockworks integration.** The polling decision is deliberate; this commission does not introduce or rely on event-dispatch substrate.
- **Any change to the existing engine-cascade stuck path** (where a rig's engine failure transitions the writ to stuck). That path is untouched; Spider's auto-unstick logic simply skips writs whose sub-slot cause is absent.

## References

- **Design subtree** (this commission): click `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385` and its concluded children.
- **Prior substrate commissions this one depends on** (both dispatched separately before this one):
  - Link-substrate rename sweep — introduces the `kind` field, `listKinds()`, and the dot-form id separator that `spider.follows` uses.
  - Status convention on writs — introduces the `status: Record<PluginId, unknown>` observation slot that `status.spider` lives in.
- **Status-convention design** (referenced by the prior brief; relevant here for the sub-slot semantics Spider consumes): click `c-mo33duvq-7f774446c6c9` and its concluded children.
- **Final kind naming** (`spider.follows`): click `c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3` (name + description), with the id-format adjustment at `c-mo3465sf-d9187bc9322a`.
- **Visibility-gap follow-up** (parked): click `c-mo301yp9-c186db746a77`.",
    "codex": "nexus",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-17T20:38:26.048Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-17T20:38:26.048Z"
  }
]

Wire the Spider so that writ dispatch can be gated on outbound links of a load-bearing kind that expresses precedence. Recovery (a previously failed dependency eventually succeeding) auto-unsticks the dependent. Cycles in the dependency graph are detected by Spider and surfaced as a stuck condition. Two pieces of substrate have been landed in prior commissions — the link-meaning layer (renamed to link-*kinds* with `label`/`kind` fields) and the per-plugin observation slot on writs (`status.<pluginId>`). **Description (verbatim):** *"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."*

Spider registers the kind through the existing `supportKit.linkKinds` contribution mechanism; Clerk consumes it through the existing kit substrate.

### Composition: conjunctive

Multiple outbound `spider.follows` links from a single writ are conjunctive — *all* targets must release before the dependent dispatches. Any single non-terminal blocker keeps the gate closed; any single failed blocker puts the dependent into `stuck`.

### Direction: outbound from the dependent

A writ's own dependencies are its *outbound* `spider.follows` links (source → target means "source depends on target"). Spider reads the candidate writ's outbound links and inspects the target writs' phases. "Inbound on the target" and "outbound on the source" are the same edges; the outbound reading matches the directional semantics and avoids inverting the kind's meaning at the read site.

### Re-evaluation: Spider's poll

Gate state is re-evaluated on Spider's existing crawl loop. No new event-dispatch substrate is required. The same poll that picks up newly-eligible writs also re-walks the dependencies of currently-gated and currently-stuck writs.

### Cycle handling: Spider detects, Clerk stays thin

Cycles in the `spider.follows` graph are detected by Spider during gate evaluation, not prevented by Clerk at link-creation. Clerk remains link-kind-agnostic. Cycle detection runs per-evaluation (during the same graph walk that evaluates gates), not as a separate periodic scan. A cycle puts every writ in the cycle into `stuck` with a clear cycle-related reason; cycles are recoverable (when one member transitions out via external action, the others auto-unstick on the next poll).

When a previously-failed blocker eventually reaches success (typically via operator-driven retry), Spider auto-unsticks the gated dependent on the next poll, returning it to `open`. The gated state is purely derived from blocker state; once the cause is resolved, the symptom is resolved.

### Provenance: Spider records its own causes

Spider only auto-unsticks writs whose stuck transition Spider itself authored. The mechanism is the per-plugin observation slot established in the prior status-convention commission: Spider writes its stuck-cause provenance into its own sub-slot (`status.spider`). | Blocker state | Gate behavior |
|---|---|
| `completed` | Release the gate (contributes to the "all blockers terminal and successful" predicate). |
| `cancelled` | Treated the same as `completed` for gate-release purposes. Cancellation is an operator-chosen terminal outcome; dependent writs should not stuck-cascade from it. |
| `failed` | Cascade to `stuck` on the dependent with `stuckCause = 'failed-blocker'`. |

Transitive cascade (sticking writs that depend on the newly-stuck one) is not done as a single atomic pass — the next Spider poll naturally evaluates those writs against their own blockers and sticks them through the same mechanism. | `stuck` → `open` (auto-unstick) | All previously-failed blockers eventually reached terminal success (or the cycle was broken). Spider clears its `status.spider.stuckCause`. |

## Intent

Wire the Spider so that writ dispatch can be gated on outbound links of a load-bearing kind that expresses precedence. A writ that depends on others is held until those others reach a terminal state; success releases the gate, non-success terminal states put the dependent into `stuck`. Recovery (a previously failed dependency eventually succeeding) auto-unsticks the dependent. Cycles in the dependency graph are detected by Spider and surfaced as a stuck condition. This is the first real consumer of the link-kind substrate and the first writer of the per-plugin observation slot on writs.

## Motivation

Two pieces of substrate have been landed in prior commissions — the link-meaning layer (renamed to link-*kinds* with `label`/`kind` fields) and the per-plugin observation slot on writs (`status.<pluginId>`). Both are in place but unused. No plugin currently reacts to a kind. No plugin currently writes into the observation slot.

The Spider currently dispatches every `open` writ of a dispatchable type as soon as concurrency allows, with no awareness of relationships between writs. Any real workflow with ordering (build before deploy, parent before child wrap-up, one commission depends on another, etc.) has to rely on the operator authoring writs in a specific sequence and never benefits from the substrate that exists for exactly this purpose.

Spider is the natural first consumer because it already owns the dispatch decision. The questions that needed answering — composition, re-evaluation mechanism, cycle handling, recovery semantics, provenance — were worked through in the click subtree at `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385`.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### The kind: `spider.follows`

A new link-kind contributed by the Spider plugin.

**Description (verbatim):** *"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."*

The kind is a pure temporal-ordering contract. Spider's specific policy (stuck-on-failure, auto-unstick on recovery) is its own consumer-side behavior, not part of the kind's meaning. A future consumer with different recovery semantics could legitimately bind to the same kind.

Spider registers the kind through the existing `supportKit.linkKinds` contribution mechanism; Clerk consumes it through the existing kit substrate.

### Composition: conjunctive

Multiple outbound `spider.follows` links from a single writ are conjunctive — *all* targets must release before the dependent dispatches. Any single non-terminal blocker keeps the gate closed; any single failed blocker puts the dependent into `stuck`.

### Direction: outbound from the dependent

A writ's own dependencies are its *outbound* `spider.follows` links (source → target means "source depends on target"). Spider reads the candidate writ's outbound links and inspects the target writs' phases. "Inbound on the target" and "outbound on the source" are the same edges; the outbound reading matches the directional semantics and avoids inverting the kind's meaning at the read site.

### Re-evaluation: Spider's poll

Gate state is re-evaluated on Spider's existing crawl loop. No new event-dispatch substrate is required. The same poll that picks up newly-eligible writs also re-walks the dependencies of currently-gated and currently-stuck writs.

### Cycle handling: Spider detects, Clerk stays thin

Cycles in the `spider.follows` graph are detected by Spider during gate evaluation, not prevented by Clerk at link-creation. Clerk remains link-kind-agnostic. Cycle detection runs per-evaluation (during the same graph walk that evaluates gates), not as a separate periodic scan. A cycle puts every writ in the cycle into `stuck` with a clear cycle-related reason; cycles are recoverable (when one member transitions out via external action, the others auto-unstick on the next poll).

### Auto-unstick on recovery

When a previously-failed blocker eventually reaches success (typically via operator-driven retry), Spider auto-unsticks the gated dependent on the next poll, returning it to `open`. Rationale: the operator's action *was* fixing the dependency; requiring a second manual unstick step is redundant ceremony. The gated state is purely derived from blocker state; once the cause is resolved, the symptom is resolved.

### Provenance: Spider records its own causes

Spider only auto-unsticks writs whose stuck transition Spider itself authored. The mechanism is the per-plugin observation slot established in the prior status-convention commission: Spider writes its stuck-cause provenance into its own sub-slot (`status.spider`). This cleanly separates Spider-authored stucks from stucks caused by other actors (operator, future plugins, engine cascade), which Spider will not touch.

- `types.ts` — `CrawlResult` discriminated union (`collected` | `grafted` | `dispatched` | `spawned` | `blocked`), `SpiderConfig`, `RigDoc`, `EngineInstance`. Will need a new `CrawlResult` variant for gating outcomes if we want parity with `blocked` (see D4). Also the place where a `SpiderStatusDoc` shape (shape of `status.spider`) belongs if we emit a typed export (see D1).
- `spider.ts` — main crawl loop. Key sites:
  - `crawl()` — priority order is **collect > processGrafts > checkBlocked > run > spawn**. `trySpawn` is where we hook gating; `checkBlocked` (different concept — rig-level blocker checks) is unrelated to `spider.follows`. Naming collision worth noting but not resolved here.
  - `trySpawn()` (~line 1788) — loops open writs of dispatchable types, checks for an existing rig, calls `fabricator.spawnRig`. Insertion point for gating: *after* the "existing rig" early-return, *before* rig creation, for each candidate writ.
  - CDC on rigs book (~line 2172) — when `rig.status === 'stuck'`, transitions writ to `stuck` with `resolution = failedEngine?.error`. Does **not** write `status.spider`. This is the engine-cascade path that auto-unstick must ignore.
  - `supportKit` declaration (~line 2031) — `requires: ['stacks', 'clerk', 'fabricator']`, `consumes: ['blockTypes', 'rigTemplates', 'rigTemplateMappings']`. Add `linkKinds: [{ id: 'spider.follows', description: '...' }]` here.
- `block-types/writ-phase.ts` — reference for reading writs via `stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs')`. Same pattern for reading blocker phases during gate evaluation.
- `spider.test.ts` — existing test rig. Uses in-memory fabricator and clerk; gate tests will extend this pattern.
- `oculus-routes.ts` — Spider's own Oculus route contributions (`/api/spider/config`, session transcript/stream). Unrelated to this commission but confirms the existing route-contribution mechanism.

### Clerk (`packages/plugins/clerk/src/`)

- `types.ts` — `WritDoc.status?: Record<string, unknown>`, `WritPhase` union, `WritLinkDoc { sourceId, targetId, label, kind }`, `KindEntry`, `LinkKindDoc`, `ClerkApi`, `ClerkKit { writTypes?, linkKinds? }`.
- `clerk.ts`:
  - `ClerkKit` interface (line ~59) — `linkKinds?: LinkKindDoc[]` contribution field.
  - `registerKitLinkKinds` (line ~217) — validates id format (kebab-case suffix after dot), enforces plugin-id prefix match, dedupes. Hard-fails on malformed. `spider.follows` is prefix-matched to `spider`, suffix `follows` — both pass.
  - `link()` (line ~401) — upserts `WritLinkDoc`. Validates `kind` against the kit registry. Composite id: `{sourceId}:{targetId}:{normalized-label}`.
  - `listKinds()` (line ~496) — returns registered `LinkKindDoc[]`. Consumed by Oculus to populate the dropdown.
  - `setWritStatus(writId, pluginId, value)` (line ~592) — atomic read-modify-write preserving sibling sub-slots. Single writer API for `status.<pluginId>`. This is the only supported path for Spider to record its provenance.
  - Phase transition validation — `ALLOWED_FROM` governs which phases can go to which. `open → stuck` and `stuck → open` are both allowed; Spider's gating path relies on both.
- `tools/writ-link.ts` — already accepts `kind?` param.
- `tools/writ-link-kinds.ts` — tool returns `{ kinds: LinkKindDoc[] }` or raw array with `json: true`. Route: `GET /api/writ/link-kinds`. Consumed by Oculus.

### Oculus writ page (`packages/plugins/clerk/pages/writs/index.html`)

- Single-page HTML+JS. ~1291 lines. Self-contained module; vanilla JS against `fetch`.
- Datalist at line ~130 — `<datalist id="link-labels">` with preset labels (retries/supersedes/fixes/duplicates/blocks). Not affected by this commission; the dropdown is additive.
- Add-link form at line ~504-509 — target-id input + label input + Link button. Needs a `kind` dropdown inserted alongside (ordering/placement is a cosmetic decision).
- `doAddLink()` (line ~758) — POSTs to `/api/writ/link` with `{ sourceId, targetId, label }`. Needs to include `kind` (or omit when blank).
- API helper (line ~158) — standard fetch wrapper. Error surfaces already support inline display.
- Kinds fetcher — *new code*. Call `/api/writ/link-kinds` on page load; populate a `<select>` with `<option value="">(no kind)</option>` + one option per registered kind.

## Adjacent patterns

- **Contributing load-bearing metadata from a kit.** `writTypes` (Clerk) and `blockTypes` / `rigTemplates` / `rigTemplateMappings` (Spider) are existing kit-contribution shapes. `linkKinds` (Clerk) is the same pattern, already implemented. Spider's addition is purely declarative.
- **Reading writs from Spider.** `block-types/writ-phase.ts` reads via `stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').get(id)`. Same pattern for reading blocker phases.
- **Writing writ phase from Spider.** The existing engine-cascade path in the rigs-book CDC handler uses `clerk.updateWritPhase(writId, 'stuck', { resolution })`. Same path is used for gating stuck (plus an added `setWritStatus` call for provenance) and auto-unstick (`stuck → open`, plus a `setWritStatus` clearing the cause).
- **Short-id formatting.** `ratchet/src/tools/click-tree.ts` line ~18 defines `shortId(id) = id.split('-').slice(0, 2).join('-')`. Same formatting is needed for the human-readable resolution text (e.g. `w-mo3depkw`). Either import or replicate — replication is trivial and avoids cross-plugin dep (see D2).
- **Per-plugin status worked example.** `docs/architecture/apparatus/clerk.md` line ~452 shows the canonical example `setWritStatus(writ.id, 'spider', { stuckCause: 'engine-failed', ... })`. **Note:** the doc's example uses `engine-failed` as a stuckCause value — but the brief prescribes `status.spider` is *not* written on the engine-cascade path. Doc/code discrepancy flagged in observations.
- **Oculus dropdown-from-API.** No existing precedent inside the writ page for a kit-registry-sourced dropdown; the datalist-from-static-array is the closest. The pattern will be novel in this file but trivially simple (fetch + map to `<option>`).

## Existing context & click lineage

- **Design subtree** — `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385` and children. All concluded. Questions settled here include composition (conjunctive), direction (outbound-on-dependent), cycle handling (per-evaluation during graph walk), recovery semantics (auto-unstick), provenance (`status.spider`), terminal-state treatment per state (completed/cancelled release, failed cascades, non-terminal holds).
- **Name + description** — `c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3`. Concluded with the final `spider.follows` name and the prescribed description text.
- **Id-format adjustment** — `c-mo3465sf-d9187bc9322a`. Concluded with the dot-separator form (`spider.follows`, not `spider:follows`).
- **Status-convention design** — `c-mo33duvq-7f774446c6c9`. Concluded: the `status: Record<string, unknown>` slot on writs; single-writer-per-sub-slot rule; `setWritStatus` transactional API. This commission is the first live writer.
- **No-DB-migration confirmation** — `c-mo33e1vs`. Concluded: the status field is implicit in existing Stacks storage; no schema change needed.
- **Parked: visibility gap** — `c-mo301yp9-c186db746a77`. `open` overloads "gated / eligible / running." Explicitly out of scope. Do not treat as a decision. If inventory touches Oculus writ-page rendering, stop at the add-link form.
- **Prior substrate commissions (both dispatched before this one)**:
  - Link-substrate rename — introduced `kind`, `listKinds()`, dot-separator id convention.
  - Status convention — introduced `WritDoc.status` and `ClerkApi.setWritStatus`.

Both are assumed landed and verified by this commission's brief. No coordination needed.

## Tool/route mapping

Clerk's `writ-link-kinds` tool maps to `GET /api/writ/link-kinds` via the Instrumentarium's `toolNameToRoute` (first hyphen splits resource/action). The Oculus page calls this route directly; no new route or tool is needed.

- `docs/architecture/apparatus/clerk.md` line ~452 uses `engine-failed` as an example `stuckCause` value in the Spider sub-slot. The doc's example is illustrative of *shape* but slightly mismatched with the prescribed writer convention. Noted in observations; no change to the doc in this commission (not in scope).

358	  "observations": "# Observations\n\n## Future follow-ups implied by this commission\n\n### Oculus gate-state column (parked concern)\nThe brief parks the open-phase visibility gap at `c-mo301yp9`, and the natural remedy is an Oculus writ-table column that reads from `status.spider`. This commission establishes the sub-slot shape that future work depends on (decision D1). When that follow-up commission lands, the `status.spider` reader will be a second-party reader of the same shape we're about to write for the first time — exactly the blast radius considered under razor criterion 4. If the patron anticipates needing more than `stuckCause` for the column (e.g., a \"gated\" indicator without stucking), revisit D1 now rather than after Oculus has wired up readers.\n\n### Stale resolution on auto-unstick\nWhen Spider auto-unsticks a writ (`stuck → open`, clearing `status.spider.stuckCause`), the writ's `resolution` field still contains the old human text (\"Blocked by failed dependency: w-abc123\"). `resolution` is not currently a per-phase field — it's written on terminal-like transitions but never cleared. After auto-unstick, the writ is `open` but carries a stale resolution naming the (now-successful) former blocker. Two reasonable stances:\n\n- **Acceptable historical breadcrumb.** `resolution` is read as \"most recent stuck reason,\" and a released writ carrying one is just lineage.\n- **Bug.** A writ in `open` with a \"Blocked by...\" resolution is semantically wrong; the auto-unstick path should clear or update it.\n\nNot in scope for this commission (the brief does not prescribe behavior either way), but worth patron awareness. A minimal fix would be: on auto-unstick, overwrite `resolution` with something like \"Auto-unstuck; blockers resolved.\" A follow-up commission could also reframe `resolution` as a phase-change audit trail rather than a single field.\n\n### Two distinct Spider→stuck paths with distinct conventions\nThis commission adds a second Spider→stuck transition path (gating) that writes `status.spider.stuckCause`. The existing engine-cascade path (rigs-book CDC → writ stuck) does *not* write the sub-slot by design. That asymmetry is load-bearing (auto-unstick uses absence as a signal) but not necessarily obvious to a reader of the code. Consider a brief comment on the engine-cascade CDC handler explaining why it stays silent on `status.spider`. Observation, not implementation request — the brief already makes the decision explicit.\n\nLonger-term, as more plugins write into `status.<pluginId>`, the engine-cascade path may deserve its own provenance marker (e.g. `status.spider.stuckCause = 'engine-failed'` to make the asymmetry purely a \"Spider owns all its stuck writes\" rule rather than \"Spider writes some stucks and not others\"). The doc example in `clerk.md` line ~452 already implies this future shape (see doc/code discrepancy below).\n\n## Doc/code discrepancies\n\n### `clerk.md` sub-slot example vs. brief prescription\n`docs/architecture/apparatus/clerk.md` line ~452 shows a worked example of `setWritStatus(writ.id, 'spider', { stuckCause: 'engine-failed', ... })`. The brief prescribes that the engine-cascade path does *not* write the sub-slot in this commission. The doc example is illustrating *shape*, not writer convention, but a future reader could easily take it as prescriptive. A one-line clarification in the doc (\"this is an example of *what Spider could write*, not what it currently does\") would prevent confusion. Not in scope for this commission — the brief does not ask for doc changes.\n\n## Performance / scaling notes (not bugs)\n\n### No index on `status.<pluginId>` sub-slot\nDecision D5 auto-decides in favor of `scan-all-stuck` for auto-unstick because stuck-writ counts are bounded by operator attention (stuck writs are noticed and resolved). If the guild grows to a regime where hundreds of writs are simultaneously stuck with Spider-authored causes, the scan becomes wasteful on every poll. A follow-up commission could add a stacks index on `status.<pluginId>.stuckCause`. Not needed today.\n\n### Per-candidate DFS vs whole-graph precompute\nDecision D3 auto-decides in favor of per-candidate DFS because the graph is expected to be small. If rig-graph density grows (many writs with many outbound `spider.follows` each), the per-candidate walk does redundant work across candidates on the same poll. Whole-graph SCC precompute on each crawl tick would amortize this, at the cost of more upfront work per poll. Not needed today.\n\n## Code-quality opportunities noticed in passing\n\n### Naming collision: `checkBlocked` vs gating as \"blocked\"\nSpider's existing crawl priority includes a `checkBlocked` phase that is unrelated to `spider.follows` gating — it checks rig-level block-types (writ-phase etc.). The brief speaks of \"blocked\" dispatch for the gate. Decision D4 resolves this by introducing a distinct `gated` `CrawlResult` variant, but the underlying naming remains: two different concepts of \"blocked\" coexist in the code. Possible follow-up: rename `checkBlocked` to `checkRigBlockers` (or similar) to disambiguate. Not in scope.\n\n### `spider.ts` size\n`spider.ts` is 2,262 lines, which already feels like it's carrying multiple responsibilities (main loop, CDC handlers, rig lifecycle, block-type registry). Adding gate-evaluation code will extend it further. A future refactor could split by responsibility — `crawl-loop.ts`, `cdc-handlers.ts`, `gate.ts`, etc. Mentioning for the punch list; not recommended as part of this commission.\n\n### Oculus writ page size\n`packages/plugins/clerk/pages/writs/index.html` is a 1,291-line single file mixing HTML, CSS, and JS. Adding the kind dropdown is additive and low-risk, but the file is pushing the maintainable limit for a vanilla-JS SPA. No refactor recommended here; this commission should be careful to follow the existing conventions even if they are cramped.\n\n## Substrate-consumer observations\n\n### First writer vs. \"first-party precedent\"\nThis commission is the first live writer of *any* `status.<pluginId>` sub-slot. That means whatever shape Spider chooses (D1) becomes the *de facto* convention the next consumer looks at as precedent. If the enriched-on-stuck shape is adopted, subsequent plugins will likely mirror it (field order, field names, timestamp format). This is a light form of vocabulary-setting that the razor's reach test covers for D1 — but worth keeping in mind when the patron reviews D1.\n\n### Clerk tool `writ-link-kinds` has a `json: true` flag\nClerk's `writ-link-kinds` tool already supports a `json: true` mode returning the raw array, vs the default human-readable table. The Oculus page needs the raw shape. 1	/**
2	 * The Clerk — writ lifecycle management apparatus.
3	 *
4	 * The Clerk manages the lifecycle of writs: lightweight work orders that flow
5	 * through a fixed status machine (new → open → completed/failed/cancelled,
6	 * with stuck as a non-terminal "needs attention" state off open).
7	 * Each writ has a type, a title, a body, and optional codex and resolution
8	 * fields.
9	 *
10	 * Writ types are validated against the guild config's writTypes field plus the
11	 * built-in type ('mandate'). An unknown type is rejected at post time.
12	 * Kits may also contribute writ types via their writTypes field.
13	 *
14	 * See: docs/architecture/apparatus/clerk.md
15	 */

1	/**
2	 * Clerk public types.
3	 *
4	 * All types exported from @shardworks/clerk-apparatus.
5	 */
6	
7	// ── Writ status ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8	
9	/**
10	 * A writ's position in its lifecycle.
11	 *
12	 * Transitions:
13	 *   new    → open       (publish)   — draft enters the queue
14	 *   new    → cancelled  (cancel)
15	 *   open   → completed  (complete)
16	 *   open   → failed     (fail)
17	 *   open   → cancelled  (cancel)
18	 *   open   → stuck      (engine failure cascade)
19	 *   stuck  → open       (recovery/retry resumes execution)
20	 *   stuck  → failed     (obligation abandoned)
21	 *   stuck  → cancelled  (obligation withdrawn)
22	 *
23	 * completed, failed, cancelled are terminal — no further transitions.
24	 * stuck is non-terminal — it represents a "needs attention" state.
25	 */
26	export type WritStatus = 'new' | 'open' | 'stuck' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled';
27	
28	// ── Documents ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
29	
30	/**
31	 * A writ document as stored in The Stacks.
32	 */
33	export interface WritDoc {
34	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
35	  [key: string]: unknown;
36	  /** Unique writ id (`w-{base36_timestamp}{hex_random}`). Sortable by creation time. */
37	  id: string;
38	  /** Writ type — must be a type declared in guild config, or a built-in type. */
39	  type: string;
40	  /** Current lifecycle status. */
41	  status: WritStatus;
42	  /** Short human-readable title. */
43	  title: string;
44	  /** Detail text. */
45	  body: string;
46	  /** Target codex name. */
47	  codex?: string;
48	  /** Parent writ id. Absent on root writs. Immutable after creation. */
49	  parentId?: string;
50	  /** ISO timestamp when the writ was created. */
51	  createdAt: string;
52	  /** ISO timestamp of the last mutation. */
53	  updatedAt: string;
54	  /** ISO timestamp when the writ reached a terminal state. */
55	  resolvedAt?: string;
56	  /** Summary of how the writ resolved (set on any terminal transition). */
57	  resolution?: string;
58	}
59	
60	// ── Requests ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
61	
62	/**
63	 * Request to post a new commission (create a writ).
64	 */
65	/**
66	 * Request to edit a writ. Title and body can be edited in any status.
67	 * Type and codex can only be changed while the writ is in 'new' status.
68	 * All fields are optional — only provided fields are updated.
69	 */
70	export interface EditWritRequest {
71	  /** Writ id. */
72	  id: string;
73	  /** New title. */
74	  title?: string;
75	  /** New body text. */
76	  body?: string;
77	  /** New writ type. Must be a valid declared type. */
78	  type?: string;
79	  /** New target codex name. Pass empty string to clear. */
80	  codex?: string;
81	}
82	
83	export interface PostCommissionRequest {
84	  /**
85	   * Writ type. Defaults to the guild's configured defaultType, or "mandate"
86	   * if no default is configured. Must be a valid declared type.
87	   */
88	  type?: string;
89	  /** Short human-readable title describing the work. */
90	  title: string;
91	  /** Detail text. */
92	  body: string;
93	  /** Optional target codex name. */
94	  codex?: string;
95	  /**
96	   * When true, the writ is created in 'new' (draft) status instead of 'open'.
97	   * Draft writs are invisible to the Spider and must be explicitly published
98	   * (new → open) before they can be picked up for execution.
99	   * Defaults to false (writ enters the queue immediately).
100	   */
101	  draft?: boolean;
102	  /**
103	   * Create this writ as a child of the specified parent writ.
104	   * The parent must be in new or open status.
105	   */
106	  parentId?: string;
107	}
108	
109	// ── Filters ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
110	
111	/**
112	 * Filters for listing writs.
113	 */
114	export interface WritFilters {
115	  /** Filter by status. Accepts a single status or an array of statuses (OR). */
116	  status?: WritStatus | WritStatus[];
117	  /** Filter by writ type. Accepts a single type or an array of types (OR). */
118	  type?: string | string[];
119	  /** Filter to children of this parent writ. */
120	  parentId?: string;
121	  /** Maximum number of results (default: 20). */
122	  limit?: number;
123	  /** Number of results to skip. */
124	  offset?: number;
125	}
126	
127	// ── Configuration ───────────────────────────────────────────────
128	
129	/**
130	 * A writ type entry declared in clerk config.
131	 */
132	export interface WritTypeEntry {
133	  /** The writ type name (e.g. "mandate", "task", "bug"). */
134	  name: string;
135	  /** Optional human-readable description of this writ type. */
136	  description?: string;
137	}
138	
139	/**
140	 * Clerk apparatus configuration — lives under the `clerk` key in guild.json.
141	 */
142	export interface ClerkConfig {
143	  /** Additional writ type declarations. The built-in type "mandate" is always valid. */
144	  writTypes?: WritTypeEntry[];
145	  /** Default writ type when commission-post is called without a type (default: "mandate"). */
146	  defaultType?: string;
147	}
148	
149	// Augment GuildConfig so `guild().guildConfig().clerk` is typed without
150	// requiring a manual type parameter at the call site.
151	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
152	  interface GuildConfig {
153	    clerk?: ClerkConfig;
154	  }
155	}
156	
157	// ── Link documents ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
158	
159	/**
160	 * A link document as stored in The Stacks (clerk/links book).
161	 *
162	 * A writ link has two complementary identifiers for its relationship:
163	 *
164	 *   - `type` is a casual, human-facing label. It is an open string, normalized
165	 *     at write time via the link-type normalization pipeline (lowercase,
166	 *     trim, camelCase split, snake_case/kebab-case split, whitespace
167	 *     collapse). Variant spellings of the same label collapse to a single
168	 *     canonical form; distinct labels remain distinct. Normalization is
169	 *     purely syntactic — it is NOT synonymy. `requires` and `depends on`
170	 *     normalize to different strings; callers that want to treat them as the
171	 *     same relationship should attach the same `semanticMeaning` to both.
172	 *
173	 *   - `semanticMeaning` is the load-bearing identifier. It is a stable,
174	 *     plugin-owned id drawn from the kit-contributed meaning registry, and
175	 *     identifies the specific relationship a downstream consumer wants to
176	 *     react to (rendering parent/child, gating publish on `refines`, etc.).
177	 *     `null` when the caller treated this link as a casual label.
178	 */
179	export interface WritLinkDoc {
180	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
181	  [key: string]: unknown;
182	  /** Deterministic composite key: `{sourceId}:{targetId}:{normalized type}`. */
183	  id: string;
184	  /** The writ that is the origin of this relationship. */
185	  sourceId: string;
186	  /** The writ that is the target of this relationship. */
187	  targetId: string;
188	  /**
189	   * Casual relationship label — an open string normalized syntactically at
190	   * write time (e.g. "fixes", "retries", "supersedes", "duplicates",
191	   * "depends on"). NOT a synonymy layer. For load-bearing identity, use
192	   * `semanticMeaning`.
193	   */
194	  type: string;
195	  /**
196	   * Load-bearing identifier for this relationship. When non-null, references
197	   * a registered meaning id from the `linkMeanings` kit registry and is the
198	   * field downstream consumers key on. Always present on each row (`null`
199	   * when the caller did not attach a meaning).
200	   */
201	  semanticMeaning: string | null;
202	  /** ISO timestamp when the link was created. */
203	  createdAt: string;
204	}
205	
206	// ── Link meaning registry ────────────────────────────────────────────
207	
208	/**
209	 * A link-meaning contribution declared by a kit's `linkMeanings` array.
210	 *
211	 * Kit authors use this shape when populating `ClerkKit.linkMeanings`. Meaning
212	 * ids must be prefixed with the contributing plugin's id followed by `:` and
213	 * a kebab-case suffix — e.g. kit `astrolabe` contributing `astrolabe:refines`.
214	 * The registry-projection view (returned by `listMeanings()`) is `MeaningDoc`,
215	 * which embeds the resolved owner plugin id; kit authors do not repeat their
216	 * plugin id on each entry.
217	 */
218	export interface MeaningEntry {
219	  /**
220	   * Fully-qualified meaning id. Must begin with `{pluginId}:` and be followed
221	   * by a non-empty kebab-case suffix (lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens,
222	   * not starting or ending with a hyphen).
223	   */
224	  id: string;
225	  /** Human-readable description of the relationship this meaning denotes. */
226	  description: string;
227	}
228	
229	/**
230	 * A link-meaning record as projected by `listMeanings()`.
231	 *
232	 * Unlike `MeaningEntry`, this shape embeds the resolved owner plugin id so
233	 * consumers of the registry do not need to parse it back out of `id`.
234	 */
235	export interface MeaningDoc {
236	  /** Fully-qualified meaning id. */
237	  id: string;
238	  /** Plugin id of the kit that contributed this meaning. */
239	  ownerPlugin: string;
240	  /** Human-readable description of this meaning. */
241	  description: string;
242	}
243	
244	/**
245	 * Result of querying links for a writ — both directions in one response.
246	 */
247	export interface WritLinks {
248	  /** Links where this writ is the source (this writ → other writ). */
249	  outbound: WritLinkDoc[];
250	  /** Links where this writ is the target (other writ → this writ). */
251	  inbound: WritLinkDoc[];
252	}
253	
254	// ── Writ type metadata ──────────────────────────────────────────────
255	
256	/**
257	 * Metadata for a registered writ type, returned by listWritTypes().
258	 */
259	export interface WritTypeInfo {
260	  /** The writ type name. */
261	  name: string;
262	  /** Human-readable description, or null if none was provided. */
263	  description: string | null;
264	  /** Origin of this type: "builtin", "guild", or the contributing plugin id. */
265	  source: string;
266	  /** Whether this is the guild's default writ type. */
267	  isDefault: boolean;
268	}
269	
270	// ── API ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
271	
272	/**
273	 * The Clerk's runtime API — retrieved via guild().apparatus<ClerkApi>('clerk').
274	 */
275	export interface ClerkApi {
276	  /**
277	   * Post a new commission, creating a writ in 'open' status by default.
278	   * If `request.draft` is true, the writ is created in 'new' status instead
279	   * and will not be picked up by the Spider until explicitly published.
280	   * Validates the writ type against declared types in guild config.
281	   */
282	  post(request: PostCommissionRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;
283	
284	  /**
285	   * Show a writ by id. Throws if not found.
286	   */
287	  show(id: string): Promise<WritDoc>;
288	
289	  /**
290	   * Resolve a writ id prefix to the full id. Mirrors ratchet's resolveId
291	   * for clicks — callers may pass the short display form (e.g. `w-mo2xi6pt`)
292	   * and receive the full id back. Throws when no writ matches or when the
293	   * prefix is ambiguous.
294	   */
295	  resolveId(prefix: string): Promise<string>;
296	
297	  /**
298	   * List writs with optional filters, ordered by createdAt descending.
299	   */
300	  list(filters?: WritFilters): Promise<WritDoc[]>;
301	
302	  /**
303	   * Count writs matching optional filters.
304	   */
305	  count(filters?: WritFilters): Promise<number>;
306	
307	  /**
308	   * Transition a writ to a new status, optionally setting additional fields.
309	   * Validates that the transition is legal.
310	   */
311	  transition(id: string, to: WritStatus, fields?: Partial<WritDoc>): Promise<WritDoc>;
312	
313	  /**
314	   * Create a typed directional link from one writ to another.
315	   * Both writs must exist. Self-links are rejected. The `type` argument is
316	   * normalized before the composite id is constructed, so variant spellings
317	   * of the same label collapse to a single link. When a link with the same
318	   * canonical composite id already exists, the existing row is returned;
319	   * if `semanticMeaning` is supplied, it is written onto the existing row
320	   * (upsert). The optional `semanticMeaning` must reference an id registered
321	   * in the kit-contributed meaning registry; unknown ids are rejected.
322	   */
323	  link(
324	    sourceId: string,
325	    targetId: string,
326	    type: string,
327	    semanticMeaning?: string,
328	  ): Promise<WritLinkDoc>;
329	
330	  /**
331	   * Query all links for a writ — both outbound (this writ is the source)
332	   * and inbound (this writ is the target).
333	   */
334	  links(writId: string): Promise<WritLinks>;
335	
336	  /**
337	   * Remove a link. Idempotent — no error if the link does not exist.
338	   */
339	  unlink(sourceId: string, targetId: string, type: string): Promise<void>;
340	
341	  /**
342	   * Edit a writ. Title and body can be edited in any status.
343	   * Type and codex can only be changed while the writ is in 'new' status.
344	   * Only the provided fields are updated. Validates type against
345	   * declared writ types if provided.
346	   */
347	  edit(request: EditWritRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;
348	
349	  /**
350	   * List all registered writ types with metadata.
351	   * Returns builtin types, guild-configured types, and kit-contributed types.
352	   * Each entry includes the type name, optional description, source, and
353	   * whether it is the default type.
354	   */
355	  listWritTypes(): WritTypeInfo[];
356	
357	  /**
358	   * List all registered link meanings contributed by kits.
359	   * Returns the registry-projection view of each meaning, including its
360	   * owner plugin id. Returns an empty array when no meanings have been
361	   * registered.
362	   */
363	  listMeanings(): Promise<MeaningDoc[]>;
364	}

content.text:
Alright, I've got the lay of the land. Here's the assessment.

## Reality check — the plan is accurate, but not against `main`

The plan's inventory reads like fiction if you look at `/workspace/nexus` on `main` — there's no `linkKinds`, no `setWritStatus`, no `WritPhase`, no `status: Record<PluginId, unknown>` slot. Clerk still has `linkMeanings`, `listMeanings`, `semanticMeaning`, `astrolabe:refines` (colon).

But that's the wrong frame of reference. This commission runs on a **stacked draft branch** (`draft-mo3detn9-71ae606a`), which has three freshly-landed commits on top of `main`:

- `62451cc refactor(clerk): rename link-substrate vocabulary (label / kind)` — the rename sweep
- `75de531 refactor(clerk): split writ spec/status — rename status→phase, add plugin observation slot` — status convention
- `f56beee refactor(clerk): address review findings on spec/status split` — review follow-up

On the draft branch the substrate *is* there: `setWritStatus()`, `listKinds()`, `linkKinds`, dot-form ids, `WritPhase` type, `WritDoc.status?: Record<string, unknown>` — all present. Tests exist for them. The plan's line numbers and API names line up.

**Verdict: the plan is a faithful reading of its own working tree.** The two prior commissions in the brief's references are committed but not yet merged to `main` — which is the normal stacked-branch topology for the autonomous pipeline, not a defect.

One minor thing worth noting: the Status Convention commission actually did *two* things — it added the observation slot **and** renamed the lifecycle field from `status` to `phase`. The plan and brief both speak fluently in the post-rename vocabulary, so nothing is broken, but if you're mentally comparing to `main` you'll trip on this.

## Open questions

Of the 22 decisions, **D10–D22 are pre-empted** (brief prescribed them or prior clicks settled them) — no action needed. **D1–D9 are open** in the sense that the plan has `recommendation` but no `selected`. Of those, only **D1** is deliberately surfaced for patron review; D2–D9 are auto-decided under the razor/three-defaults heuristic and are effectively "confirm or override."

### D1 — Shape of `status.spider` (the real question)

| Option | What gets written | Cost | Future-ready? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `minimal` | `{ stuckCause }` on stuck | Minimal | Locks out any reader that needs more without a migration |
| `enriched-on-stuck` *(planner pick)* | `{ stuckCause, blockerIds, observedAt }` on stuck; nothing while gated | Writes only on stuck transitions | Structured detail the brief already implies; future readers get it "for free" |
| `enriched-always` | Write on every poll while gated *and* while stuck; clear on release | Poll-cost churn; conflates "have an opinion" with "bad state" | Enables an Oculus gate-column to read without re-walking the graph |

The real trade-off is downstream: the brief parks an Oculus "gated-or-running" column at `c-mo301yp9`. When that column ships, it either **reads `status.spider`** (favors `enriched-always`) or **re-walks outbound links per row** (favors `enriched-on-stuck`).

I think the planner is right. A gate-column can cheaply compute "is this writ gated?" with a **one-hop** outbound-link inspection per row — it doesn't need a pre-computed index. Re-walking outbound `spider.follows` links is trivially fast for UI-scale row counts. So `enriched-always` pays poll churn for a convenience that isn't needed.

Also, this being the *first live writer* of any `status.<pluginId>` sub-slot, whatever shape you pick becomes the de-facto precedent for Animator/Hopper/etc. The `enriched-on-stuck` pattern — "write structured detail only when there's something to say" — is a good precedent. `enriched-always` invites every future plugin to churn the slot on every tick.

**Recommendation: `enriched-on-stuck`.**

### D2–D9 — The auto-decided cluster

All of these are planner picks I'd accept as-is. Briefly:

| # | Question | Pick | Call it? |
|---|---|---|---|
| D2 | Short-id helper: replicate vs import vs hoist | `replicate` | Accept — 3 lines, no cross-plugin dep justified |
| D3 | Cycle detection algo | `dfs-visiting-set` | Accept — correctly distinguishes cycles from diamonds |
| D4 | New `CrawlResult` variant vs reuse `blocked` | `new-gated` | Accept — different semantics, clearer telemetry |
| D5 | Finding stuck writs for auto-unstick | `scan-all-stuck` | Accept — bounded by operator attention; index is follow-up |
| D6 | Crawl-loop placement of gate eval | `separate-phase` | Accept — keeps open→stuck and stuck→open visibly distinct |
| D7 | Dropdown placement | `kind-then-label` | Accept — cosmetic, reversible |
| D8 | Empty-kind label text | `(no kind)` | Accept — explicit, matches codebase style |
| D9 | When Oculus fetches kinds | `on-page-load` | Accept — small stable payload, zero interactive latency |

The only one I'd even pause on is D4, and only to note: the planner's own inventory already flags a naming collision (`checkBlocked` is about rig-level blocks, not gating). Introducing a `gated` variant rather than reusing `blocked` helps keep that separation clean — but it also means future readers see *three* concepts in the Spider code that each overload a "blocked-ish" idea (rig-blocks, gating, `spider.follows`). Worth living with; the alternative (reuse `blocked`) is worse.

## Next steps

If you're happy with all of the above, the review action is:
- Accept `enriched-on-stuck` on **D1**
- Accept the planner's recommendations on **D2–D9**

Say the word and I can write up a quick review note you can paste into whatever wraps this up, or just confirm and let the pipeline move on. Anything you want to probe harder on D1 first?

# Wrap-Up: Complete Work and Generate Session Summary

## Generate Summary

Generate a structured summary of the current session. This summary serves one audience:

**The Scribe** — as input for session notes and published material.

Session-to-session continuity is now carried by **clicks** (see Coco's agent file and the clicks skill). Before generating the wrap-up summary, conclude or park any clicks touched this session — the click tree is what the next Coco session will read for orientation, not this wrap-up file.

<expected-format>
# Session Summary

## What we did

- Bulleted list of concrete actions taken (files created/modified, decisions made, commits pushed). Reference specific files and commit hashes.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- What was decided, including explicit "we chose NOT to" decisions. Brief rationale for each.

## Next steps & open questions

- Concrete actions for the next session or for agents to pick up.
- Open questions which were raised but not answered

### Notable moments

Capture quotes, insights, or exchanges that stand out — either for system design or for the research/experiment narrative.

For each notable moment:
- Include **direct quotes from Sean** when available
- Flag **why it's notable** (e.g., "shifted the project's core abstraction", "surfaced a tension between X and Y", "revealed an assumption about how agents should work")
- Prioritize moments where the human changed direction, challenged an assumption, or articulated something new about the project's purpose

### Experiment observations

Flag anything relevant to active experiments. Omit this section if nothing applies.

<expected-format>

## Complete Work

After wrapping up, **ALWAYS**:

- Commit any changes you have made, if any
- Push main to the remote

## Reminders

Check for things Sean should be nudged about:

- **Ethnographer interview:** Check the most recent interview file in `experiments/ethnography/interviews/` (files named `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md`). If the most recent one is more than a day old — or if none exist yet — remind Sean to run an ethnographer session. Keep it light: *"It's been a couple days since your last ethnographer interview — might be a good time to check in."*

## Guidelines

- Keep the whole summary under ~500 words. Brevity is a feature.
- Do not editorialize beyond the "why it's notable" flags.
- Write the summary to `experiments/ethnography/session-notes/new/<timestamp>.md`. where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one. This file is for the ethnographer and must NOT be auto-deleted — it accumulates until the ethnographer reviews it.
- Commit all `session-notes`, and `experiments/data/transcripts/` changes to git and push those changes. The transcripts directory may contain files from concurrent sessions — commit whatever is there.
- In addition to writing the summary, share it in the chat for review and inclusion in session transcripts

Committed and pushed. Here's the summary for the transcript:

---

# Session Summary

## What we did

- Reviewed the Astrolabe plan for `w-mo3depkw-0d1587e47150` ("Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`") against reality.
- Confirmed the plan is **faithful to its working tree** (the stacked draft branch `draft-mo3detn9-71ae606a`) — not to `main`. The two prior substrate commissions (Link-Substrate Rename, Status Convention) are committed on the draft stack but unmerged to `main`, so a reader inspecting `/workspace/nexus` on `main` will see `linkMeanings`/`semanticMeaning`/`status`-as-lifecycle while the plan speaks in `linkKinds`/`kind`/`phase`-plus-`status`-slot. Normal stacked-branch topology, not a defect.
- Walked Sean through the 22 decisions: **D10–D22** pre-empted; **D1–D9** open. **D1** (shape of `status.spider`) is the only one deliberately surfaced.
- Recommended: `enriched-on-stuck` on D1; accept planner picks on D2–D9 as-is.
- Flagged that this is the *first live writer* of any `status.<pluginId>` sub-slot, so D1's shape becomes de-facto precedent.
- Session note + coco-log entry committed as `a023fc4` and pushed to main.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Deferred to Sean's review step:** D1–D9 — Coco's recommendations captured in chat; no `selected` yet committed in the plan.
- **No clicks opened or concluded.** Purely advisory session.

## Next steps & open questions

- Sean accepts/overrides D1–D9 in the Astrolabe review surface, then the plan moves from `reviewing` to the next stage.
- Open: whether the eventual Oculus gate-column (parked at `c-mo301yp9`) will read from `status.spider` or re-walk outbound links. D1 recommendation assumes re-walk; a read-from-slot preference later may require shape expansion.

### Notable moments

- **"please checkout the plandoc for w-mo3depkw-0d1587e47150 and see how it matches reality"** — Sean framed the task as a reality-check on plan fidelity rather than yes/no on recommendations. Notable because it's a concrete X008 pattern: the patron audits the plan against the codebase rather than rubber-stamping a pre-filtered decision list. The framing surfaced the stacked-branch topology subtlety (plan accurate against draft, not main) that a narrower "which option" framing wouldn't have.

### Experiment observations

- **X008 (Criteria-Internalization):** Clean instance of mediated plan review — Coco did the codebase audit, Sean stayed at the decision layer.
- **Planner-quality signal:** The plan correctly auto-decides 8 of 9 open decisions under razor/three-defaults and deliberately surfaces the one that earns surfacing (D1 — inter-component persisted-data shape). That's exactly the discrimination the recent razor codification (commits `b003c39`, `f641362`) was aiming for. Positive evidence the auto-decide heuristic is calibrated.

---

**Nudge:** Your last ethnographer interview was `2026-04-07T132554.md` — about 10 days ago. Might be a good time to check in when you have a spare slot.
